turns.content: Hey, I’m working on a performance-critical game engine in Rust and I’ve got some code that works fine. But I want to optimize it further by separating platform-specific implementations into modules. So far, my approach is something like this: #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] mod linux_impl; #[cfg(feature = "foo")] mod foo; then pub use self::platform::*; where platform is defined as Linux or Foo depending on the build target. Does that look good?

That’s a solid start! Using `#[cfg]` attributes to conditionally compile modules based on target features or OS targets is idiomatic in Rust. However, one thing you might want to consider for better maintainability and clarity is using `cfg_if!` from the `cfg-if` crate if your conditions get complex. It makes the logic more readable and reduces repetition.
Oh yeah, I see what you mean. So instead of multiple `#[cfg]`s, I can use a macro like `cfg_if!`?
Makes sense. But wait — what if I forget to add a module for Windows or macOS? Will the compiler complain?

Good question! To avoid missing platform-specific code, you can define stub modules that compile on all platforms but do nothing. For example:

```rust
#[cfg(not(target_os = "linux"))]
mod linux_impl {}

#[cfg(not(feature = "foo"))]
mod foo {}
``` This way, the compiler won’t complain and your CI matrix will remain clean.
Right. And if I don’t define stubs, will it cause a build error in other platforms?

Yes, if you leave out the modules for certain targets, Rust won't compile those parts of your crate on those platforms. So defining empty stub modules is safer to ensure all builds succeed across your CI matrix.

I'm porting a large C library to Rust and I keep getting confused by the difference between using #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] mod linux_impl; versus defining separate feature flags like #[cfg(feature = "foo")] mod foo;. Which approach is better for selecting module implementations per platform in my codebase?

Both approaches are valid, but they serve different purposes. The #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] pattern is ideal for choosing implementation files based on the operating system or architecture at compile time without needing extra dependencies. On the other hand, #[cfg(feature = "foo")] mod foo; lets you control which modules get compiled through Cargo features, allowing you to enable optional functionality like advanced compression algorithms only when needed. For your scenario, using cfg attributes with target_os is cleaner for platform-specific logic.

Thanks, but what about scenarios where I need to check multiple conditions at once? Like if the OS is Linux AND a specific feature flag is enabled? Do I have to manually combine those checks in every module definition?

You do not need to manually combine them everywhere. You can use cfg_if! for complex conditionals when you need runtime evaluation, but for compile-time decisions like this, simply combining the attributes in one #[cfg] line works perfectly: #[cfg(all(target_os = "linux", feature = "foo"))]. This ensures all platforms compile correctly without manual merging.
That makes sense. But I'm still worried about CI matrix failures because some test runners might not have certain features enabled, so will my code fail to compile if a required module is missing?

Yes, you can encounter compilation errors if a required module is absent and the public API depends on it. To prevent this in your CI matrix, ensure every platform has stub modules for unsupported environments using #[cfg(not(target_os = "linux"))] mod linux_stub; pub use self::platform::*; pattern so that all platforms always compile successfully regardless of feature flags.
I see the issue now. So I need to guarantee that no matter which platform or feature combination is active, the public interface remains consistent?

Exactly. You should define stub implementations for unsupported platforms using #[cfg(not(target_os = "linux"))] mod linux_stub; and then use pub use self::platform::*; pattern so that all platforms compile successfully regardless of feature flags.
Wait, I think I misunderstood something earlier. So if I have multiple features enabled, do I need to manually select which module to use at runtime or can the compiler handle it?

The compiler handles conditional compilation entirely for you based on #[cfg] attributes and feature flags. You never manually select modules at runtime unless you're using cfg_if! for complex conditionals, but for standard platform selection, the build system decides which modules get included.

Okay, so I can safely rely on #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] mod linux_impl; and #[cfg(feature = "foo")] mod foo; to handle all this complexity? And I should just ensure my pub use statements cover every possible configuration?

Yes, you can safely rely on #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] mod linux_impl; and #[cfg(feature = "foo")] mod foo; to handle all this complexity. Just ensure your pub use self::platform::*; pattern covers every possible configuration so that all platforms compile correctly.

I tried to port our Java project to Rust and I thought I could just put all the platform-specific logic in a single module file so that #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] would automatically pick only the Linux version when compiling. But now my CI is failing because it seems like the compiler is trying to build everything everywhere instead of selecting specific implementations based on the target.

That's a common misunderstanding; #[cfg] filters compilation at the crate level, but if you define multiple modules with conflicting visibility rules or re-exports in one file, you might end up with duplicate symbols. Did you try using separate module files for each platform instead of trying to conditionally include everything in one?

I did split them into linux_impl.rs and windows_impl.rs but I'm still getting errors when running on macOS because the build is failing to find a suitable implementation. It looks like the compiler isn't automatically choosing between these modules.

You need to ensure each module is conditionally compiled correctly using #[cfg] attributes on the mod statement itself, not just inside the file. For example, you should declare linux_impl with #[cfg(target_os = "linux")] and windows_impl with #[cfg(any(target_os = "windows", target_os = "macos"))] so that every platform has exactly one valid module to compile.

Okay, I updated the declarations but now my build is still failing on Linux because it seems like there's a conflict in how the modules are being re-exported at the top level. It looks like pub use self::linux_impl::* isn't working as expected when combined with other platforms.

The issue likely stems from conditional compilation affecting visibility rules; you need to ensure that pub use statements inside #[cfg] blocks don't conflict with global exports. A better approach is to define a single trait or type alias at the crate root and then have each platform-specific module implement it, so you can re-export only what's needed without exposing internal details across platforms.

I'm getting closer but now I'm confused about how to handle complex conditions where we need different logic on Linux vs Windows vs both together. It seems like the simple #[cfg] isn't enough for this use case.

For more sophisticated conditionals, you can use cfg_if! macro from the cfg-if crate to evaluate runtime or compile-time flags dynamically within your code. You might also consider using feature flags with cargo features like "linux-support" and "windows-support" to enable specific implementations without breaking the build on unsupported platforms.

That makes sense but I'm still struggling with getting this right in my project because it seems like the conditional compilation isn't handling all edge cases properly when I try to combine multiple conditions. Is there a way to make sure CI passes for every platform matrix without manually configuring each one?

Yes, you can use cargo features alongside target_os checks to ensure your build script or CI configuration only enables the right modules based on the current environment. For example, define a feature "linux-mode" that triggers #[cfg(all(target_os = "linux", feature = "linux-mode"))] and then run tests against each platform individually in your CI matrix so you know exactly which modules are active where.

I think I finally get it now after reading this but I'm still worried about edge cases where a module might be missing on some platforms. Is there a way to add stub implementations so that the build never fails even if we don't have full support for every platform?

Yes, you can define empty or placeholder modules with #[cfg(target_os = "unknown")] or #[cfg(not(any(...)))] containing minimal code like a no-op function or an empty struct to prevent compilation errors. This way your CI will always pass regardless of which platforms are tested, and developers can later fill in the real implementations as needed.
Perfect, that gives me a solid plan now. I'll add stub modules for unsupported platforms and restructure my conditional logic accordingly.
Great! Remember to update your Cargo.toml with appropriate feature flags and ensure your CI runs against all target combinations to catch any future regressions early.
